content:
The knowledge that provides understanding of this world and how it works is science. The branch of science which deals with the composition, structure, properties and reactions of matter is called chemistry. It deals with every aspect of our life. The development of science and technology has provided us a lot of facilities in daily life through petrochemical products, medicines, drugs, soap, detergents, paper, plastics, paints, pigments, insecticides and pesticides. Chemistry also helps improve health, environment and conservation of natural resources. In this chapter we study different branches of chemistry, basic definitions and important concepts.

Branches of Chemistry. Chemistry is divided into several main branches including physical chemistry, organic chemistry, inorganic chemistry, biochemistry, industrial chemistry, nuclear chemistry, environmental chemistry and analytical chemistry. Physical Chemistry deals with the relationship between the composition and physical properties of matter along with the changes in them. It includes study of structure of atoms, formation of molecules, behavior of gases, liquids and solids, and effects of temperature and radiation on matter.

metadata.topic: Introduction and Physical Chemistry

content:
Physical Chemistry deals with the relationship between the composition and physical properties of matter along with the changes in them. It includes study of structure of atoms, formation of molecules, behavior of gases, liquids and solids, and effects of temperature and radiation on matter.

Organic Chemistry is the study of covalent compounds of carbon and hydrogen called hydrocarbons and their derivatives. Organic compounds occur naturally as well as synthesized in laboratories. Organic chemists determine their structure and properties. This branch covers petroleum, petrochemicals and pharmaceutical industries.

Inorganic Chemistry deals with the study of all elements and their compounds except hydrocarbons and their derivatives. It has applications in glass, cement, ceramics and metallurgy industries.

metadata.topic: Organic and Inorganic Chemistry

content:
Inorganic Chemistry deals with the study of all elements and their compounds except hydrocarbons and their derivatives. It has applications in glass, cement, ceramics and metallurgy industries.

Biochemistry is the branch of chemistry that studies the structure, composition and chemical reactions of substances found in living organisms. It covers chemical processes such as synthesis and metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins and fats in living systems. It has important applications in medicine, food science and agriculture.

Industrial Chemistry deals with manufacturing of chemical compounds on commercial scale such as oxygen, chlorine, ammonia, caustic soda, nitric acid and sulphuric acid which serve as raw materials for fertilizers, soap, textiles, paints and paper industries.

metadata.topic: Biochemistry and Industrial Chemistry

content:
Nuclear Chemistry is the branch of chemistry that deals with the radioactivity, nuclear processes and properties. The main concern of this branch is with the atomic energy and its uses in daily life. It also includes the study of the chemical effects resulting from the absorption of radiation within animals, plants and other materials. It has vast applications in medical treatment such as radiotherapy, preservation of food and generation of electrical power through nuclear reactors.

Environmental Chemistry is the branch of chemistry in which we study about components of the environment and the effects of human activities on the environment. Environmental chemistry is related to other branches like biology, geology, ecology, soil and water. The knowledge of chemical processes taking place in environment is necessary for its improvement and protection against pollution.

metadata.topic: Nuclear Chemistry and Environmental Chemistry

content:
Environmental Chemistry is the branch of chemistry in which we study about components of the environment and the effects of human activities on the environment. Environmental chemistry is related to other branches like biology, geology, ecology, soil and water. The knowledge of chemical processes taking place in environment is necessary for its improvement and protection against pollution.

Analytical Chemistry is the branch of chemistry that deals with separation and analysis of a sample to identify its components. The separation is carried out prior to qualitative and quantitative analysis. Qualitative analysis provides the identity of a substance. Quantitative analysis determines the amount of each component present in the sample. This branch covers different techniques and instruments used for analysis in food, water, environmental and clinical fields.

metadata.topic: Environmental Chemistry and Analytical Chemistry

content:
Analytical Chemistry is the branch of chemistry that deals with separation and analysis of a sample to identify its components. The separation is carried out prior to qualitative and quantitative analysis. Qualitative analysis provides the identity of a substance. Quantitative analysis determines the amount of each component present in the sample. This branch covers different techniques and instruments used for analysis in food, water, environmental and clinical fields.

Basic Definitions. Matter is simply defined as anything that has mass and occupies space. Our bodies as well as all the things around us are examples of matter. In chemistry we study all types of matter that can exist in any of three physical states solid, liquid or gas. A piece of matter in pure form is termed as a substance. Every substance has a fixed composition and specific properties or characteristics. Whereas impure matter is called a mixture which can be homogeneous or heterogeneous in its composition.

metadata.topic: Analytical Chemistry and Basic Definitions of Matter

content:
Whereas impure matter is called a mixture which can be homogeneous or heterogeneous in its composition.

Every substance has physical as well as chemical properties. The properties associated with the physical state of the substance are called physical properties like colour, smell, taste, hardness, shape of crystal, solubility, melting or boiling points. For example when ice is heated it melts to form water. When water is further heated it boils to give steam. In this entire process only the physical states of water change whereas its chemical composition remains the same.

The chemical properties depend upon the composition of the substance. When a substance undergoes a chemical change its composition changes and new substances are formed. For example decomposition of water is a chemical change as it produces hydrogen and oxygen gases. All materials are either a substance or a mixture. Matter is classified into mixtures and substances. Mixtures are further classified as homogeneous mixtures and heterogeneous mixtures. Substances are classified as elements and compounds.

metadata.topic: Basic Definitions - Physical and Chemical Properties, Classification of Matter

content:
Matter is classified into mixtures and substances. Mixtures are further classified as homogeneous mixtures and heterogeneous mixtures. Substances are classified as elements and compounds.

Elements. In the early ages only nine elements were known. Modern definition of an element is that it is a substance made up of same type of atoms having same atomic number and cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means. Each element is made up of unique type of atoms that have very specific properties.

Elements occur in nature in free or combined form. They are found in different percentages in the earth crust, oceans and atmosphere. Elements may be solids, liquids or gases. Majority of the elements exist as solids. A few elements exist as liquids such as mercury and bromine. A few elements exist as gases such as nitrogen, oxygen, chlorine and hydrogen. On the basis of their properties elements are divided into metals, non-metals and metalloids. About eighty percent of the elements are metals.

metadata.topic: Classification of Matter and Elements

content:
On the basis of their properties elements are divided into metals, non-metals and metalloids. About eighty percent of the elements are metals.

Elements are represented by symbols which are abbreviations for the name of elements. A symbol is taken from the name of that element in English, Latin, Greek or German. If it is one letter it will be capital. In case of two letters symbol only first letter is capital.

The unique property of an element is valency. It is combining capacity of an element with other elements. It depends upon the number of electrons in the outermost shell. In simple covalent compounds valency is the number of hydrogen atoms which combine with one atom of that element or the number of bonds formed by one atom of that element. In simple ionic compounds valency is the number of electrons gained or lost by an atom of an element to complete its octet. A radical is a group of atoms that have some charge. Some elements show more than one valency that is they have variable valency.

metadata.topic: Elements - Symbols and Valency

content:
Some elements show more than one valency that is they have variable valency.

Compound is a substance made up of two or more elements chemically combined together in a fixed ratio by mass. As a result of this combination elements lose their own properties and produce new substances called compounds that have entirely different properties. Compounds cannot be broken down into their constituent elements by simple physical methods. For example carbon dioxide is formed when carbon and oxygen combine chemically in a fixed ratio. Similarly water is a compound formed by chemical combination between hydrogen and oxygen in a fixed ratio of 1:8 by mass.

Compounds can be classified as ionic or covalent. Ionic compounds do not exist in independent molecular form. They form a three dimensional crystal lattice in which each ion is surrounded by oppositely charged ions. Ionic compounds have high melting and boiling points. These compounds are represented by formula units. The covalent compounds mostly exist in molecular form. A molecule is a true representative of the covalent compound and its formula is called molecular formula.

metadata.topic: Compounds - Definition and Classification

content:
Compounds can be classified as ionic or covalent. Ionic compounds do not exist in independent molecular form. They form a three dimensional crystal lattice in which each ion is surrounded by oppositely charged ions. Ionic compounds have high melting and boiling points. These compounds are represented by formula units. The covalent compounds mostly exist in molecular form. A molecule is a true representative of the covalent compound and its formula is called molecular formula.

Mixture. When two or more elements or compounds mix up physically without any fixed ratio they form a mixture. On mixing the component substances retain their own chemical identities and properties. The mixture can be separated into its parent components by physical methods such as distillation, filtration, evaporation, crystallisation or magnetization. Mixtures that have uniform composition throughout are called homogeneous mixtures for example air, gasoline and ice cream. Heterogeneous mixtures are those in which composition is not uniform throughout for example soil, rock and wood.

metadata.topic: Compounds and Introduction to Mixtures

content:
Mixtures that have uniform composition throughout are called homogeneous mixtures for example air, gasoline and ice cream. Heterogeneous mixtures are those in which composition is not uniform throughout for example soil, rock and wood.

A compound is formed by chemical combination of atoms of the elements in a fixed ratio. The constituents lose their identity and form a new substance having entirely different properties. Compounds always have fixed composition by mass. The components cannot be separated by physical means. Every compound is represented by a chemical formula. Compounds have homogeneous composition and sharp fixed melting points.

A mixture is formed by simple mixing of the substances. The mixture shows the properties of the constituents. Mixtures do not have fixed composition. The components can be separated by simple physical methods. A mixture consists of two or more components and does not have any chemical formula. They may be homogeneous or heterogeneous in composition and do not have sharp fixed melting points.

metadata.topic: Mixtures and Difference between Compound and Mixture

content:
A mixture is formed by simple mixing of the substances. The mixture shows the properties of the constituents. Mixtures do not have fixed composition. The components can be separated by simple physical methods. A mixture consists of two or more components and does not have any chemical formula. They may be homogeneous or heterogeneous in composition and do not have sharp fixed melting points.

Atomic Number and Mass Number. The atomic number of an element is equal to the number of protons present in the nucleus of its atoms. It is represented by symbol Z. As all atoms of an element have the same number of protons in their nuclei they have the same atomic number. Hence each element has a specific atomic number termed as its identification number. For example all hydrogen atoms have one proton so their atomic number is 1. All atoms of carbon have six protons so their atomic number is 6.

The mass number is the sum of number of protons and neutrons present in the nucleus of an atom. It is represented by symbol A. It is calculated as A equals Z plus number of neutrons. Each proton and neutron has one atomic mass unit mass.

metadata.topic: Mixtures Difference and Atomic Number Mass Number

content:
The mass number is the sum of number of protons and neutrons present in the nucleus of an atom. It is represented by symbol A. It is calculated as A equals Z plus number of neutrons. Each proton and neutron has one atomic mass unit mass.

Relative Atomic Mass and Atomic Mass Unit. The mass of an atom is too small to be determined practically. However certain instruments enable us to determine the ratio of the atomic masses of various elements to that of carbon-12 atoms. This ratio is known as the relative atomic mass of the element. The relative atomic mass of an element is the average mass of the atoms of that element as compared to one twelfth the mass of an atom of carbon-12 isotope. Based on carbon-12 standard the mass of an atom of carbon is twelve units and one twelfth of it comes to be one unit. The unit for relative atomic masses is called atomic mass unit with symbol amu. One atomic mass unit is one twelfth the mass of one atom of carbon-12. When expressed in grams it is 1.66 times 10 to the power minus 24 grams.

metadata.topic: Atomic Number Mass Number and Relative Atomic Mass

content:
The unit for relative atomic masses is called atomic mass unit with symbol amu. One atomic mass unit is one twelfth the mass of one atom of carbon-12. When expressed in grams it is 1.66 times 10 to the power minus 24 grams.

How to Write a Chemical Formula. Compounds are represented by chemical formulae as elements are represented by symbols. Chemical formulae of compounds are written keeping the following steps in consideration. Symbols of two elements are written side by side in the order of positive ion first and negative ion later. The valency of each ion is written on the right top corner of its symbol. This valency of each ion is brought to the lower right corner of other ion by cross-exchange method. If the valencies are same they are offset and are not written in the chemical formula. But if they are different they are indicated as such at the same position. If an ion is a combination of two or more atoms called radical bearing a net charge on it then the net charge represents the valency of the radical. The chemical formula of such compounds is written by placing the negative radical within parenthesis.

metadata.topic: Relative Atomic Mass and How to Write Chemical Formula

content:
If an ion is a combination of two or more atoms called radical bearing a net charge on it then the net charge represents the valency of the radical. The chemical formula of such compounds is written by placing the negative radical within parenthesis.

Empirical Formula. Chemical formulae are of two types. The simplest type of formula is empirical formula. It is the simplest whole number ratio of atoms present in a compound. The empirical formula of a compound is determined by knowing the percentage composition of a compound. For example the covalent compound silica has simplest ratio of 1:2 of silicon and oxygen respectively. Therefore its empirical formula is SiO2. Glucose has simplest ratio 1:2:1 of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen respectively. Hence its empirical formula is CH2O.

As discussed earlier the ionic compounds exist in three dimensional network forms. Each ion is surrounded by oppositely charged ions in such a way to form electrically neutral compound. Therefore the simplest unit taken as a representative of an ionic compound is called formula unit. It is defined as the simplest whole number ratio of ions as present in the ionic compound. In other words ionic compounds have only empirical formulae.

metadata.topic: How to Write Chemical Formula and Empirical Formula

content:
Therefore the simplest unit taken as a representative of an ionic compound is called formula unit. It is defined as the simplest whole number ratio of ions as present in the ionic compound. In other words ionic compounds have only empirical formulae.

Molecular Formula. Molecules are formed by the combination of atoms. These molecules are represented by molecular formulae that show actual number of atoms of each element present in a molecule of that compound. Molecular formula is derived from empirical formula by the following relationship: Molecular formula equals empirical formula multiplied by n where n is 1, 2, 3 and so on. For example molecular formula of benzene is C6H6 which is derived from the empirical formula CH where the value of n is 6.

The molecular formula of a compound may be same or a multiple of the empirical formula. Some compounds may have same empirical and molecular formula for example water H2O and hydrochloric acid HCl.

metadata.topic: Empirical Formula and Molecular Formula

content:
The molecular formula of a compound may be same or a multiple of the empirical formula. Some compounds may have same empirical and molecular formula for example water H2O and hydrochloric acid HCl.

Molecular Mass and Formula Mass. The sum of atomic masses of all the atoms present in one molecule of a molecular substance is its molecular mass. For example molecular mass of chlorine Cl2 is 71.0 amu, of water H2O is 18 amu and that of carbon dioxide CO2 is 44 amu. Some ionic compounds that form three dimensional solid crystals are represented by their formula units. Formula mass in such cases is the sum of atomic masses of all the atoms present in one formula unit of a substance. For example formula mass of sodium chloride is 58.5 amu and that of calcium carbonate CaCO3 is 100 amu.

The relationship between mole and mass can be expressed as number of moles equals known mass of a substance divided by molar mass of the substance. Or mass of substance in grams equals number of moles multiplied by molar mass.

metadata.topic: Molecular Formula and Molecular Mass Formula Mass

content:
The relationship between mole and mass can be expressed as number of moles equals known mass of a substance divided by molar mass of the substance. Or mass of substance in grams equals number of moles multiplied by molar mass.

Ions. Ion is an atom or group of atoms having a charge on it. The charge may be positive or negative. There are two types of ions: cations and anions. An atom or group of atoms having positive charge on it is called cation. Cations are formed when atoms lose electrons from their outermost shells. For example sodium ion Na+ and potassium ion K+ are cations.

An atom or group of atoms that has a negative charge on it is called anion. Anions are formed by the gain or addition of electrons to an atom. For example chloride ion Cl- and oxide ion O2-.

metadata.topic: Molecular Mass Formula Mass and Ions Cations Anions

content:
An atom or group of atoms that has a negative charge on it is called anion. Anions are formed by the gain or addition of electrons to an atom. For example chloride ion Cl- and oxide ion O2-.

Molecular Ion. When a molecule loses or gains an electron it forms a molecular ion. Hence molecular ion or radical is a species having positive or negative charge on it. Like other ions they can be cationic molecular ions if they carry positive charge or anionic molecular ions if they carry negative charge. Cationic molecular ions are more abundant than anionic molecular ions. For example CH4+, He+, N2+.

When gases are bombarded with high energy electrons in a discharge tube they ionize to give molecular ions. A molecule is the smallest particle of an element or compound which can exist independently and shows all the properties of that compound. It is always neutral. It is formed by the combination of atoms. It is a stable unit.

metadata.topic: Ions and Molecular Ions

content:
When gases are bombarded with high energy electrons in a discharge tube they ionize to give molecular ions. A molecule is the smallest particle of an element or compound which can exist independently and shows all the properties of that compound. It is always neutral. It is formed by the combination of atoms. It is a stable unit.

Free Radicals. Free radicals are atoms or group of atoms possessing odd number of unpaired electrons. It is represented by putting a dot over the symbol of an element for example H•, Cl•, H3C•. Free radicals are generated by the homolytic equal breakage of the bond between two atoms when they absorb heat or light energy. A free radical is extremely reactive species as it has the tendency to complete its octet.

Ions are the atoms which bear some charge. They exist in solution or in crystal lattice. Their formation is not affected by the presence of light. Free radicals are the atoms that have odd number of electrons. They can exist in solutions as well in air. They may form in the presence of light.

metadata.topic: Molecular Ions and Free Radicals

content:
Free radicals are the atoms that have odd number of electrons. They can exist in solutions as well in air. They may form in the presence of light.

Molecules. A molecule is the smallest unit of a substance. It shows all the properties of the substance and can exist independently. There are different types of molecules depending upon the number and types of atoms combining.

A molecule consisting of only one atom is called monoatomic molecule. For example the inert gases helium, neon and argon all exist independently in atomic form and they are called monoatomic molecules.

If a molecule consists of two atoms it is called diatomic molecule. For example hydrogen H2, oxygen O2, chlorine Cl2 and hydrogen chloride HCl.

If it consists of three atoms it is called triatomic molecule. For example H2O and CO2. If a molecule consists of many atoms it is called polyatomic. For example methane CH4, sulphuric acid H2SO4 and glucose C6H12O6.

metadata.topic: Free Radicals and Types of Molecules

content:
If a molecule consists of many atoms it is called polyatomic. For example methane CH4, sulphuric acid H2SO4 and glucose C6H12O6.

A molecule containing same type of atoms is called homoatomic molecule. For example hydrogen H2, ozone O3, sulphur S8 and phosphorus P4 are the examples of molecules formed by the same type of atoms. When a molecule consists of different kinds of atoms it is called heteroatomic molecule. For example CO2, H2O and NH3.

Avogadro’s Number and Mole. The number of particles in one mole of a substance is called Avogadro’s number. Avogadro’s Number is a collection of 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 particles. It is represented by symbol NA. Hence the 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 number of atoms, molecules or formula units is called Avogadro’s number that is equivalent to one mole of respective substance. In simple words 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 particles are equal to one mole as twelve eggs are equal to one dozen.

metadata.topic: Types of Molecules and Avogadro’s Number

content:
In simple words 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 particles are equal to one mole as twelve eggs are equal to one dozen.

To understand the relationship between the Avogadro’s number and the mole of a substance let us consider a few examples. 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 atoms of carbon are equivalent to one mole of carbon. 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 molecules of H2O are equivalent to one mole of water. 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 formula units of NaCl are equivalent to one mole of sodium chloride. Thus 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 atoms of elements or 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 molecules of molecular substance or 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 formula units of ionic compounds are equivalent to 1 mole.

A mole is defined as the amount of a substance that contains 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 number of particles atoms, molecules or formula units. It establishes a link between mass of a substance and number of particles. It is abbreviated as mol.

metadata.topic: Avogadro’s Number and Mole

content:
A mole is defined as the amount of a substance that contains 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 number of particles atoms, molecules or formula units. It establishes a link between mass of a substance and number of particles. It is abbreviated as mol.

Gram Atomic Mass, Gram Molecular Mass and Gram Formula Mass. The atomic mass of an element expressed in grams is called gram atomic mass. The molecular mass of a molecular substance expressed in grams is called gram molecular mass. The formula mass of an ionic substance expressed in grams is called gram formula mass. Scientists have agreed that Avogadro’s number of particles are present in one molar mass of a substance. Thus one gram atomic mass contains 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 atoms. One gram molecular mass contains 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 molecules. One gram formula mass contains 6.02 times 10 to the power 23 formula units. The relationship between mole and mass can be expressed as number of moles equals known mass of a substance divided by molar mass of the substance or mass of substance in grams equals number of moles multiplied by molar mass.

metadata.topic: Mole and Gram Atomic Molecular Formula Mass

content:
The relationship between mole and mass can be expressed as number of moles equals known mass of a substance divided by molar mass of the substance or mass of substance in grams equals number of moles multiplied by molar mass.

Mole-Mass Calculations. In these calculations we calculate the number of moles of a substance from the known mass of the substance with the help of following equation: Number of moles equals known mass of a substance divided by molar mass of the substance. When we rearrange the equation to calculate mass of a substance from the number of moles of a substance we get mass of substance in grams equals number of moles multiplied by molar mass.

For example to calculate the number of moles in a given mass first divide the known mass by the molar mass. This gives the number of moles directly. To find the mass from number of moles multiply the number of moles by the molar mass in grams.

metadata.topic: Gram Masses and Mole-Mass Calculations

content:
For example to calculate the number of moles in a given mass first divide the known mass by the molar mass. This gives the number of moles directly. To find the mass from number of moles multiply the number of moles by the molar mass in grams.

Mole-Particle Calculations. In these calculations we can calculate the number of moles of a substance from the given number of particles atoms, molecules or formula units. Number of moles equals given number of particles divided by Avogadro’s number. On rearranging above equation we get number of particles equals number of moles multiplied by 6.02 times 10 to the power 23.

Never calculate the number of particles directly from mass of the substance or vice versa. Always make calculations through moles. For calculations of the number of atoms in molecular compounds and the number of ions in ionic compounds first calculate the number of molecules or formula units and then calculate the number of atoms or ions.

metadata.topic: Mole-Mass and Mole-Particle Calculations

content:
Summary of Molar Calculations. The relationship between mass of a substance number of moles and number of particles is shown as mass of substances divided by molar mass gives mole. Mole multiplied by molar mass gives mass of substances. Mole multiplied by Avogadro number gives number of particles. Number of particles divided by Avogadro number gives mole. Never calculate the number of particles from mass of the substance or vice versa. Always make calculations through moles. For calculations of the number of atoms in molecular compounds and the number of ions in ionic compounds first calculate the number of molecules or formula units and then calculate the number of atoms or ions.

Unit 2 Structure of Atoms. Ancient Greek philosopher Democritus suggested that matter is composed of tiny indivisible particles called atoms. The name atom was derived from the Latin word Atomos meaning indivisible. In the beginning of 19th century John Dalton put forward Atomic Theory. According to it all matter is made up of very small indivisible particles called atoms.

metadata.topic: Molar Calculations Summary and Unit 2 Introduction

content:
Unit 2 Structure of Atoms. Ancient Greek philosopher Democritus suggested that matter is composed of tiny indivisible particles called atoms. The name atom was derived from the Latin word Atomos meaning indivisible. In the beginning of 19th century John Dalton put forward Atomic Theory. According to it all matter is made up of very small indivisible particles called atoms. Till the end of 19th century it was considered that atom cannot be subdivided. However in the beginning of 20th century experiments performed by Goldstein J J Thomson Rutherford Bohr and other scientists revealed that atom is made up of subatomic particles like electron proton and neutron.

Theories and Experiments related to Atomic Structure. Rutherford made important contributions to the development of the Atomic Theory. He performed the gold foil experiment which showed that atom has a small dense positively charged nucleus at the centre with electrons revolving around it. Most of the atom is empty space.

metadata.topic: Introduction to Atomic Structure and Rutherford Contribution

content:
Rutherford performed the gold foil experiment which showed that atom has a small dense positively charged nucleus at the centre with electrons revolving around it. Most of the atom is empty space.

Bohr atomic theory differed from Rutherford. Bohr proposed that electrons revolve around the nucleus in fixed circular paths called orbits or shells. Electrons in each shell have fixed energy. Electrons can jump from one shell to another by absorbing or emitting energy. The structure of atom includes the location of the proton electron and neutron. Proton is in the nucleus with positive charge. Neutron is in the nucleus with no charge. Electron is outside the nucleus with negative charge.

metadata.topic: Rutherford Experiment Bohr Theory and Atomic Structure

content:
Bohr atomic theory differed from Rutherford. Bohr proposed that electrons revolve around the nucleus in fixed circular paths called orbits or shells. Electrons in each shell have fixed energy. Electrons can jump from one shell to another by absorbing or emitting energy. The structure of atom includes the location of the proton electron and neutron. Proton is in the nucleus with positive charge. Neutron is in the nucleus with no charge. Electron is outside the nucleus with negative charge.

Isotopes. Isotopes are atoms of the same element having same atomic number but different mass numbers. Isotopes have same number of protons and electrons but different number of neutrons. Isotopes of an element have identical chemical properties but slightly different physical properties due to difference in mass numbers.

Isotopes are represented by writing the mass number as superscript and atomic number as subscript before the symbol of the element.

metadata.topic: Bohr Theory Atomic Structure and Isotopes Definition

content:
Isotopes are represented by writing the mass number as superscript and atomic number as subscript before the symbol of the element.

Properties of Isotopes of Hydrogen Carbon Chlorine and Uranium. Hydrogen has three isotopes: protium H-1 with one proton no neutron, deuterium H-2 with one proton one neutron, tritium H-3 with one proton two neutrons. Carbon has isotopes C-12 and C-14. Chlorine has two common isotopes Cl-35 and Cl-37. Uranium has isotopes U-235 and U-238. All isotopes of an element show same chemical behaviour because they have same number of electrons and protons. Physical properties like density melting point boiling point differ slightly due to different mass numbers.

Structure of different isotopes can be drawn from mass number and atomic number by showing number of protons neutrons in nucleus and number of electrons in shells.

metadata.topic: Isotopes and Properties of H C Cl U

content:
All isotopes of an element show same chemical behaviour because they have same number of electrons and protons. Physical properties like density melting point boiling point differ slightly due to different mass numbers.

Structure of different isotopes can be drawn from mass number and atomic number by showing number of protons neutrons in nucleus and number of electrons in shells.

Importance and Uses of Isotopes. Isotopes have vast applications in various fields of life. Radioactive isotopes are used in medicine for diagnosis and treatment of diseases such as cancer radiotherapy. In industry isotopes are used for tracing leaks in pipelines and studying reaction mechanisms. In agriculture isotopes help in studying plant metabolism and developing better fertilizers. In archaeology carbon-14 dating is used to determine age of fossils and ancient objects. Uranium-235 is used as fuel in nuclear reactors for generation of electricity.

Electronic Configuration. Electrons are arranged in she ls around the nucleus. Each shell is divided into subshells. The first shell K has only one subshell called s. Second shell L has two subshells s and p. Third shell M has three subshells s p and d. Shells are numbered as 1 2 3 etc and subshells are designated as s p d f.

metadata.topic: Isotopes Uses and Electronic Configuration Subshells
